themes.heading: Sicherheitsstufe
themes.text: country-security"> Stand 20.07.2026 (Unverändert gültig seit: 27.05.2026) Sicherheitsstufe Sicherheitsstufe 4 (regional) seit 01.01.2014 Landkarte Regionale Reisewarnung Sicherheitsstufe 4 (von 4) gilt für das Gebiet an der Grenze zu Somalia. Hohes Sicherheitsrisiko Sicherheitsstufe 3 (von 4) gilt in den „Counties“ Turkana, West Pokot, Elgeyo Marakwet, Baringo, Laikipia und Samburu. Sicherheitsrisiko Sicherheitsstufe 2 (von 4) gilt im Rest des Landes. Die Sicherheitslage kann sich jederzeit verändern und entwickeln. Daher empfehlen wir Ihnen: Nützen Sie unsere Auslandsservice-App sowie die kostenlose Reiseregistrierung! Informieren Sie sich über die Sicherheitslage vor Ort und überprüfen Sie diese regelmäßig!

themes.heading: Aktuelle Hinweise
themes.text: Seit 26. Mai 2026 werden Einreisende aus der Republik Uganda, Vereinigten Republik Tansania und der Demokratischen Republik Kongo mittels Ganzkörper-Temperaturscannern an den Flughäfen Jomo Kenyatta International Airport in Nairobi und Moi International Airport in Mombasa auf mögliche Anzeichen einer Ebola-Infektion untersucht. Bei auffälliger Temperaturerhöhung findet eine weiterführende medizinische Anamnese sowie eine klinische Untersuchung statt. Aufgrund der aktuellen militärischen Auseinandersetzungen in der Nahost- und Golfregion kann es zu zahlreichen Flugausfällen kommen. Nehmen Sie bei allfälligen Fragen direkt mit der jeweiligen Fluglinie Kontakt auf.

themes.heading: Sicherheit & Kriminalität
themes.text: Vor Besuchen von Slums und dergleichen wird gewarnt. Auch von den angebotenen „guided slum tours“ wird aus Sicherheitsgründen strengstens abgeraten. Regionale Reisewarnung (Sicherheitsstufe 4) für das Gebiet an der Grenze zu Somalia. Vor Reisen in die Counties Mandera, Wajir, Lamu und Garissa wird gewarnt. Seit 2011 werden regelmäßig terroristische Angriffe der radikal-islamischen Al-Shabaab Milizen auf Ziele in den Provinzen entlang der Grenze zu Somalia verübt. Hohes Sicherheitsrisiko (Sicherheitsstufe 3): Aufgrund der derzeitigen Unruhen im Norden des Landes wurde in den „Counties“ (Turkana/West Pokot/Elgeyo Marakwet/Baringo/Laikipia/Samburu) bis auf weiteres eine nächtliche Ausgangssperre verfügt. Sicherheitsrisiko (Sicherheitsstufe 2) für den Rest des Landes. Öffentliche Plätze, öffentliche Gebäude, von Ausländern frequentierte Restaurants, Bars und Einkaufszentren sollten nur bei unbedingter Notwendigkeit und unter äußerster Wachsamkeit besucht werden. Meiden Sie grundsätzlich Menschenansammlungen! Nach einem Anschlag ist der Tatort unverzüglich zu verlassen, da nachfolgende Bombenanschläge (nach Eintreffen der Hilfsmannschaften) häufig sind. Überfälle von Piraten aus Somalia können in den Gewässern vor Kenia nicht ausgeschlossen werden. Für aktuelle Informationen wird auf die Website des IMB Piracy Reporting Centre – ICC – Commercial Crime Services (icc-ccs.org) verwiesen. Kriminalität: Im Falle eines Überfalles sollte unter keinen Umständen Widerstand geleistet werden, da die Gewaltbereitschaft sehr hoch ist; professionelle Entführungen westlicher Ausländer mit Lösegeld-Forderungen; oft werden Entführungsopfer über längere Zeit festgehalten, um mit deren Bankkarten Geldbeträge abheben zu können. Besondere Vorsicht bei der Behebung von Geldbeträgen am Geldautomaten oder Nutzung von Kreditkarten ist geboten. Treffen Sie die üblichen Sicherheitsmaßnahmen für Auslandsreisen (wenig Bargeld, kein Schmuck, Dokumente fotokopieren) und halten Sie sich nicht nachts in abgelegenen oder dunklen Gebieten auf. Diebstähle mit Motorrad möglich! Versperren Sie immer Türen und Fenster des Fahrzeuges aufgrund des Risikos von „Car Jacking“ , auch wenn kleinere Unfälle bzw. Sachschäden am Auto herbeigeführt wurden. Unternehmen Sie Wanderungen nur in Begleitung vertrauenswürdiger Wanderführer, die z.B. dem Kenya Wildlife Service (KWS), ihrem Reisebüro oder ähnlichen Institutionen angehören! Hinweis: Reisewarnungen werden im Regelfall nur in besonderen Krisensituationen ausgesprochen, wenn eine generelle Gefährdung für Leib und Leben besteht. Reisende, die sich in ein Gebiet mit Reisewarnung begeben, müssen sich der möglichen Konsequenzen bewusst sein: Konsularische Hilfeleistung für in Not geratene Österreicherinnen und Österreicher ist nur äußerst eingeschränkt möglich Bei Reisen in Gebiete mit Reisewarnung können Versicherungen trotz eines aufrechten Versicherungsvertrages Ausschlussgründe geltend machen und sind damit leistungsfrei. Das Konsulargebührengesetz ermächtigt die Republik Österreich, die Kosten für allenfalls erforderliche Schutzmaßnahmen bzw. Hilfeleistung in bestimmten Situationen im Regressweg von Reisenden zurückzufordern! Notrufnummern: Ambulanz: 999 Feuerwehr: 122 oder 999 Polizei: 999, 112 oder 911

themes.heading: Einreise & Ausreise
themes.text: Reiseregistrierung: Eine eTA (electronic Travel Authorisation) ist für alle Reisenden verpflichtend (das System per se ist sehr fehleranfällig und es werden viele Probleme u.a. bei der Zahlung gemeldet). Es wird empfohlen, es mehrmals (eventuell mit anderen Kreditkarten) zu versuchen). Bei Einreise muss ein Ausdruck der Travel Authorisation vorgelegt werden. Visumpflicht: Nein (visumfrei bis zu maximal 90 Tage; Gültigkeit 90 Tage ab Ausstellung) Visum erhältlich: Für Aufenthalte über 90 Tage wenden Sie sich bitte an die kenianische Botschaft in Wien. Reisedokumente: Reisepass Passgültigkeit: 6 Monate bei Einreise eAusweise: Elektronische Ausweise (ID Austria) werden derzeit ausschließlich in Österreich akzeptiert. Cremefarbiger Notpass: Wird akzeptiert Konventions- oder Fremdenpass: Wenden Sie sich bitte an die weiter unten angeführte „ausländische Vertretung in Österreich“. Sonstiges: Reisende müssen ihre Wiederausreise (Rückflug- oder Weiterreise-Ticket) sowie genügend Geldmittel für den Aufenthalt nachweisen können. Bei Reisen aus einem Gelbfiebergebiet (auch Zwischenstopps von über 12 Stunden) ist eine gültige Gelbfieberimpfung vorzuweisen. Minderjährige: Für Minderjährige (bis 18 Jahre), die ohne Begleitung der gesetzlichen Vertreter oder mit nur einem gesetzlichen Vertreter verreisen, wird – zusätzlich zum eigenen Reisepass – auch eine Einverständniserklärung für die Reise benötigt. Dieser Vollmacht sollte eine Kopie der Geburtsurkunde des bzw. der Minderjährigen sowie eine Kopie der Reisepässe der gesetzlichen Vertreter angeschlossen sein. Bei unterschiedlichen Nachnamen empfiehlt sich auch die Mitnahme der Heiratsurkunde der Eltern. Muster für eine Einverständniserklärung finden Sie auf der Seite des ÖAMTC und des ARBÖ. Diebstahl/Verlust des Reisedokuments: Von der Verwendung gestohlener oder verlorener und wieder gefundener Reisedokumente wird abgeraten, auch wenn die Anzeige bei der zuständigen Behörde bereits widerrufen wurde, da Probleme an der Grenze bis zur Einreiseverweigerung nicht ausgeschlossen werden können.

themes.heading: Einfuhr & Ausfuhr
themes.text: Die Ein- und Ausfuhr von Fremdwährung und Landeswährung ist unbegrenzt erlaubt. Beträge ab umgerechnet 5.000 USD sind deklarationspflichtig. Kreditkarten werden (in den größeren Städten) akzeptiert. Es werden keine Dollarnoten angenommen, die älter als 2006 sind. Wertvolle Gegenstände sind zu deklarieren und können teilweise nur gegen Stellung einer Kaution eingeführt werden. Die Einfuhr von Waffen, Frischfleisch, Pflanzen und Obst ist verboten. Die Einfuhr und Benutzung von Plastiktaschen ist mit Strafen bis zu ca. EUR 30.000 bzw. bis zu 4 Jahren Haft belegt. Die Ausfuhr von Gold und Diamanten ist verboten. Eine besondere Genehmigung ist für Jagdtrophäen, Felle und Häute erforderlich. Die Mitnahme von CS-Gas und Pfefferspray ist verboten und wird als unerlaubter Waffenbesitz qualifiziert. Die Einfuhr von Drohnen ist nicht erlaubt. Nähere Auskünfte finden Sie auch im Travel Centre der IATA. Die angeführten Mengen und Beträge sind unverbindliche Richtangaben, rechtsverbindliche Informationen kann nur die Vertretungsbehörde dieses Landes erteilen. Bitte beachten Sie bei der Einreise nach Österreich die geltenden Einfuhrbestimmungen.

themes.heading: Gesundheit
themes.text: Krankenhäuser entsprechen nicht dem europäischen Standard. Ausführliche Informationen zu gängigen Infektionskrankheiten auf Reisen erhalten Sie auch beim Öffentlichen Gesundheitsportal Österreichs bzw. bei den tropenmedizinischen Instituten. Es besteht kein Sozialversicherungsabkommen mit Österreich. Es wird darauf hingewiesen, dass die Kosten einer allfälligen Krankenhausbehandlung erheblich sein können. Der Abschluss einer alle Risiken abdeckenden Reiseversicherung wird daher vor Reiseantritt dringend empfohlen. Die Mitnahme einer Reiseapotheke, die nicht nur regelmäßig benötigte Arzneimittel, sondern auch Medikamente für gängige Reiseerkrankungen beinhaltet, wird empfohlen. Wer auf bestimmte Medikamente angewiesen ist, sollte einen ausreichenden Vorrat und einen Nachweis über die ärztliche Verschreibung mitnehmen, auf der Homepage des Sozialministeriums finden Sie nähere Informationen zur Mitnahme von Medikamenten ins Ausland. Es wird empfohlen, rechtzeitig vor Reisebeginn den Hausarzt oder eine andere geeignete Einrichtung zu kontaktieren, um sich über eventuell erforderliche Reiseimpfungen zu erkundigen.

themes.heading: Infrastruktur
themes.text: Öffentliche Verkehrsverbindungen: ausreichende Sicherheit im Flugverkehr, vor allem am Internationalen Jomo Kenyatta Flughafen in Nairobi. Von der Benützung der öffentlichen Verkehrsmittel (Matatu-Kleinbusse, Bus) sowie der Motorrad-Taxis „Boda-Boda“ wird aufgrund der hohen Unfallgefahr und der faktischen Nichtversicherung dringend abgeraten. Benutzen Sie nur lizenzierte Taxis vor Hotels oder Flughäfen bzw. Apps (inter)nationaler Fahrtenvermittle, vermeiden Sie, Taxis auf der Straße aufzuhalten. Bei Einreise mit dem eigenen PKW ist ein „Carnet de Passage“ sowie der internationale Führerschein in Verbindung mit dem österreichischen Führerschein mitzuführen. Die Hauptverkehrsstraßen sind häufig in schlechtem Zustand. Der Straßenverkehr ist gefährlich, besonders nachts. Manche Überlandstraßen sollten nur in Begleitung einer Polizeieskorte befahren werden. Auch für geringe Verkehrsdelikte gelten drakonische Strafen (Verhaftung und Geldstrafen weit über 1.000 EUR). Die Verkehrspolizei gilt als extrem unzuverlässig, es kommt laufend zu Anhaltungen und unverschämten Forderungen „ohne Quittung“ bei gleichzeitiger Androhung von Haft. Weitere Informationen zu Verkehrsbestimmungen finden Sie auf der Länderdatenbank des ÖAMTC und des ARBÖ.

themes.heading: Umwelt
themes.text: Wechsel von Regen- und Trockenzeit. An der Küste tropisches Monsunklima (Regenzeit: Juni bis November), im Hochland gemäßigtes Klima mit kühlen Nächten (Regenzeit: November und März bis Mai).

themes.heading: Besondere Bestimmungen
themes.text: Das Fotografieren von öffentlichen Gebäuden (u.a. militärische Einrichtungen, Flughäfen, Botschaften etc.) ist verboten und kann zur Festnahme führen. Drogendelikte (selbst für den Eigenbedarf) werden hart bestraft. Homosexuelle Handlungen sind verboten und werden mit hohen Gefängnisstrafen geahndet. Das Schutzalter für sexuelle Handlungen kann gegenüber den in Österreich geltenden Bestimmungen um einige Jahre höher sein oder sogar über dem Erwachsenenalter von 18 Jahren liegen. Es können jedoch auch (beispielsweise in einzelnen Provinzen oder Regionen) unterschiedliche Bestimmungen zur Anwendung kommen.

If you’re arriving from Uganda, Et
au.levelLabel: Exercise a high degree of caution

themes.heading: Latest update
themes.text: Still current at:20 July 2026 Updated:29 May 2026 Latest update:We continue to advise exercise a high degree of caution in Kenya due to the threat of terrorism and violent crime. Higher levels apply in some areas. Protests across Kenya, including in Nairobi may block roads and can turn violent. Security forces have used tear gas to disperse crowds (see 'Safety'). If you’re arriving from Uganda, Ethiopia or the Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC), you may be subject to enhanced health screening measures due to the ongoing Ebola outbreak centred in the DRC (see 'Health' and 'Travel'). We advise: Exercise a high degree of caution in Kenya overall due to the threat of terrorism and violent crime. Higher levels apply in some areas. Download Kenya Download Africa

themes.heading: Safety
themes.text: Protests and demonstrations may block roads and can turn violent. Security forces have used tear gas to disperse crowds. Monitor local media for security updates and alerts, follow the advice of local authorities and avoid large crowds. There's an ongoing high threat of terrorist attacks in Kenya. Terrorist attacks could happen at any time. Remain vigilant when visiting public areas. Kidnapping is a risk across Kenya. The borders with Somalia, Ethiopia and South Sudan are high-risk areas. Armed groups from Somalia kidnap and target foreigners in Kenya, including aid workers, particularly along the Somalia border region. Foreigners have previously been the target of kidnappings, including express kidnappings. Violent crime occurs, including carjackings, kidnapping and home invasions. Don't walk around after dark. If you live in Kenya, invest in strong personal security measures. Scams are common. Criminals often use fake police or government identification to extort money from travellers. Be wary of anyone asking for money or information, even if they seem official. Kenya can experience extreme weather and natural disasters. There are normally two rainy seasons, from March to June and October to November. Flash flooding can occur and may damage infrastructure and disrupt essential services. National parks may be affected. Avoid walking or driving through flooded areas. Full travel advice: Safety

themes.heading: Health
themes.text: Outbreaks of Ebola disease have occurred in parts of Africa. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms. There's currently an outbreak of Mpox in parts of Africa, including in Kenya. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone who has Mpox. Malaria is widespread, except in Nairobi and places higher than 2500 metres above sea level. Consider taking anti-malarial medication. Other insect-borne diseases including dengue, Rift Valley fever, filariasis and African sleeping sickness are common. Ensure your accommodation is insect-proof. Use insect repellent. Yellow Fever is widespread. Get vaccinated before you travel and bring your vaccination certificate with you. HIV/AIDS infection rates are high. Take precautions if you're taking part in high-risk activities. You may be exposed to foodborne, waterborne and other infectious diseases, including diphtheria, cholera, hepatitis, meningococcal disease and measles. Drink only boiled or bottled water. Avoid raw or undercooked food. Full travel advice: Health

themes.heading: Local laws
themes.text: It's illegal to work or volunteer without a valid work permit. To work in the charity sector, get a valid work permit through the Directorate of Immigration Services. Same-sex relationships are illegal. Avoid public displays of affection. It's illegal to take photos of official buildings. Get advice before taking photos. It's also illegal to destroy the local currency and possess ivory. Kenya recognises dual nationality but hasn't fully enacted laws around it. If you're a dual national, always travel on your Australian passport. Foreign journalists seeking to work in Kenya must apply for accreditation through the Media Council of Kenya portal. If you plan to take part in surrogacy arrangements in Kenya, be aware of all legal and other risks involved. For further details see ‘Surrogacy’ section below. Full travel advice: Local laws

themes.heading: Travel
themes.text: If you’re arriving from Uganda, Ethiopia or DRC, you may be subject to enhanced health screening measures due to the ongoing Ebola outbreak centred in the DRC. Strikes at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport in Nairobi can occur without notice. Check with your airline or travel provider before you travel, and arrive early at the airport to ensure you have enough time to clear security and immigration. You need to apply online for an electronic travel authorisation before you travel. Entry and exit conditions can change at short notice. Check for updates on Kenyan Government websites or through the local Kenyan High Commission, Embassy or Consulate. If you're a foreign resident, always carry your alien identity card. You could be fined or detained if you don't. If you're coming from a yellow fever-endemic country, you'll need to show a yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Kenya. To access National Parks in Kenya, you'll need to pay through the eCitizen platform. Full travel advice: Travel

themes.heading: Local contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. For consular assistance, contact the Australian High Commission in Nairobi. In an emergency, Australian citizens can contact the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. To stay up to date with local information, follow the High Commission’s social media accounts. Full travel advice: Local contacts

themes.heading: Terrorism
themes.text: There's an ongoing high threat of terrorist attacks in Kenya. While attacks can occur at any time, concerns are heightened around anniversary dates of previous attacks. These include 7 August, 11 September, 21 September Westgate Mall attack, and the 7 October Hamas attack against Israel. The terrorist group al-Shabaab continues to threaten attacks. Further attacks are possible and could happen at any time, including in areas popular with foreigners and tourists. Kenyan authorities remain on high alert. Terrorist acts could include: suicide bombings shootings kidnappings roadside bomb attacks improvised explosive devices attacks on civil aviation

themes.heading: Possible attack targets
themes.text: Terrorist attacks could occur anywhere and at any time. Be alert to possible threats, especially in public places. Areas prone to terrorist attacks include: Nairobi coastal areas, including all of Lamu County, and areas of Tana River and Kilifi Counties, extending 65km inland in Tana River County and 50km inland in Kilifi County north of the Galana-Sabaki River Kenya's border regions with Somalia, Ethiopia and South Sudan, including all of Mandera and Garissa counties and the part of Wajir county extending from the town of Wajir eastwards to the border with Somalia Terrorists may target: shopping areas, including malls and markets entertainment venues, including bars, nightclubs, restaurants and cafes tourist locations including hotels, resorts, beaches and safari lodges places of worship refugee camps international facilities, including foreign embassies, UN premises and international schools government and non-government organisations (NGOs) offices and buildings, including educational institutions Terrorists may also target transport and transport infrastructure such as: airports and commercial airlines road and rail hubs and associated infrastructure maritime vessels operating in or near Kenyan ports Foreign aid workers may be specifically targeted at refugee camps near the Kenya-Somalia border. Terrorist attacks have occurred in Kenya in recent years, including in Lamu, Mandera and Garissa counties.

themes.heading: Security
themes.text: Consider likely terrorist targets and assess available security measures. Report any suspicious items or activities to police. To reduce your risks: take official warnings seriously monitor local media for threats follow the instructions of local authorities If there’s a terrorist incident, leave the area if safe to do so and follow the instructions of local authorities. Don't gather in groups after an attack. This also applies if you're evacuated from a building for security reasons, such as a bomb threat. More information: Terrorism

themes.heading: Civil unrest and political tension
themes.text: Political and social tensions can turn into unrest at short notice. Protests and events that draw large groups of people may block roads and can turn violent. These include anniversaries of large protests on 24 June, 25 June and 7 July. Demonstrations can happen in Kenya during and after international events, unpopular government policy decisions or political changes. Monitor local media for updates of planned protests and avoid large crowds. During protests, security forces may use rubber bullets, tear gas, water cannons and, at times, live ammunition, which can result in fatalities and injuries. Opportunistic crime can happen around the protest or large gathering area, including looting, robberies and assaults. Violent outbreaks are less common in tourist areas. However, riots and clashes have occurred in major urban centres, including: Mombasa Nairobi Kisumu During periods of unrest, follow these safety measures: avoid large crowds, protests and demonstrations expect heavy security measures limit your movements and avoid affected areas, especially areas around government buildings monitor local media for updates plan for interruptions to phone and internet services contact your airline or tour operator to confirm arrangements before you travel stay alert for curfews ordered by authorities at short notice follow instructions from local authorities More information: Demonstrations and civil unrest

themes.heading: Kidnapping
themes.text: Kidnapping occurs across the world with political, ideological and criminal motives. Foreigners, including Australians, have been targeted while travelling overseas. Kidnapping can happen anywhere, even in places that seem safe. Kidnapping is a risk across Kenya, but particularly in the border region with Somalia and coastal counties. Express kidnappings occur in Kenya, particularly in Nairobi, and anyone can be targeted. Criminals may hold you captive and force you to withdraw money from ATMs or banking applications on your phone. Several active terrorist groups have the intent and capability to kidnap foreigners. Somalia-based armed groups have kidnapped aid workers in the Somalia border region. While foreign nationals and residents have been targeted in coastal resorts and towns in the North Eastern region (Mandera, Wajir and Garissa counties), Lamu county and parts of Tana River and Kilifi counties. In these areas, kidnapping is a high threat for: humanitarian workers journalists tourists expats missionaries If, despite our advice, you travel to an area with a high risk of kidnapping, be aware that our ability to provide consular assistance in these destinations is limited. You’re responsible for your own safety. You can find more information on how to reduce your risk of kidnapping here: Kidnapping The Australian Government maintains a longstanding policy that it doesn’t make payments or concessions to kidnappers. Ransom payments to kidnappers have funded further terrorist attacks and criminal activity. Ransom payments to terrorist groups are likely to contravene Australian counter-terrorism financing laws. More information: Reducing the risk of kidnapping Scams that affect travellers Reducing the risk of piracy

themes.heading: Crime
themes.text: Crime rates are high across most regions of Kenya, particularly in Nairobi, Mombasa and Kisumu, and coastal beach resorts. Criminal activities increase during holiday periods. Incidents of armed robbery, carjacking and kidnapping are possible in: Nairobi and other urban centres, including Mombasa some coastal regions, including all of Lamu County, and parts of Tana River and Kilifi Counties the North Eastern region, specifically Mandera, Wajir and Garissa counties Parts of the North Rift and Central Rift regions, including Turkana, West Pokot, Elgeyo Marakwet, Baringo, Laikipia and Samburu counties

themes.heading: Petty crime
themes.text: Groups of bag-snatchers and carjackers on motorbikes target pedestrians and motorists. Thieves snatch jewellery, bags and phones from pedestrians or through open vehicle windows, most often while cars are stopped at traffic lights or in heavy traffic. Robberies also occur on trains and buses. To protect yourself from theft: hold bags and backpacks in front of you or in ways that make them harder to snatch take care crossing the street or walking along footpaths avoid wearing headphones in public to maintain situational awareness be aware of motorbikes approaching from behind as you walk on the footpath never leave your bags unsupervised be prepared to surrender your valuables rather than risk being injured in a struggle always keep vehicle doors locked, windows up and valuables out of sight, even when moving secure your accommodation, including when you're inside avoid walking after dark avoid walking in isolated back-alleys and lanes

themes.heading: Violent crime
themes.text: Violent crimes include: armed carjackings kidnapping home invasions Foreigners have been targeted in private homes in Nairobi, in tourist areas and while travelling by road. Incidents have also occurred at night outside residential security gates. Violent crime is particularly common in the Nairobi suburbs of Eastleigh and Kibera. Take extra precautions in these areas. If you're living in Kenya, invest in strong personal security measures and regularly review your personal security arrangements. Do not physically resist any robbery attempt. Police regularly confront criminal suspects in public places. Random gunfire has killed or wounded bystanders in crowded areas.

themes.heading: Attacks and sexual assault
themes.text: Sexual assaults can affect both male and female travellers. Due to widespread HIV/AIDS, if you're a victim of violent crime such as rape, see a doctor immediately. Victims of sexual assault may have difficulty receiving adequate social or medical support. While sexual assault is mostly frequently associated with females, sexual assault of men also occurs in Kenya and often goes unreported because of the stigma attached to this type of assault.

themes.heading: Crime in outlying areas
themes.text: Incidents involving banditry and cattle rustling can occur in North and Central Rift regions, including Turkana, West Pokot, Elgeyo Marakwet, Baringo, Laikipia and Samburu counties. Clashes between local groups have also occurred in the Mount Elgon region near the border with Uganda. The region bordering Somalia is extremely dangerous. Curfews and security operations can be instituted without prior notice in the North Rift and parts of the Central Rift regions. If you plan to travel to these areas get up-to-date advice on security and other conditions from your tour operator before you travel. Monitor the media for latest developments, maintain a high level of vigilance, and leave restricted areas as soon as possible.

themes.heading: Scams and fraud
themes.text: Scams are common, including those involving property ownership, short-stay rentals or business ventures. Criminals often use fake police, government and other identification to extort money from travellers. You may be threatened with arrest or false charges and pressured to make immediate cash payments, withdraw money from an ATM or make mobile money transfers. This may happen during traffic stops, at checkpoints or when seeking assistance from authorities. Learn more about staying within the law. To protect yourself: be wary of demands for money or personal information, including from people claiming to be police or officials. Always ask for and carefully check identification never send money to someone you haven’t met in person if you've already given them money, don't go overseas to try to get it back be wary of fake bank notes in circulation

themes.heading: Tours and adventure activities
themes.text: Transport and tour operators don't always follow safety and maintenance standards. This includes adventure activities, such as diving. If you plan to do an adventure activity: check if your travel insurance policy covers the activity use registered tour operators with a good reputation ask about and insist on minimum safety requirements always use available safety gear, such as life jackets or seatbelts. If proper safety equipment isn't available, use another provider. Information on tourism, road conditions and emergency regional assistance is available from the Kenya Tourism Federation Safety and Communication Centre. Phone: +254 (0) 722 074 564 5 or +254 (0) 738 617 499

themes.heading: Extreme weather and natural disasters
themes.text: Kenya can experience extreme weather and natural disasters including: earthquakes volcanic activity tsunamis flash flooding mudslides drought If a natural disaster occurs: secure your passport in a safe, waterproof place follow the advice of local authorities keep in contact with your friends and family Register with the Global Disaster Alert and Coordination System to receive alerts on major disasters.

themes.heading: Flooding
themes.text: Kenya normally has two rainy seasons: from March to June and October to November. Flash flooding and mudslides are common. Flooding may affect the following essential services: transport and infrastructure, including flights electricity supply water and food supplies emergency services If flooding occurs: monitor local news and weather reports follow the advice of local authorities ask your tour operator if tourist services at your destination have been affected secure your passport in a safe, waterproof location keep in touch with your friends and family consider moving to higher ground

themes.heading: Earthquakes and volcanoes
themes.text: Kenya lies on a fault line and sometimes experiences earthquakes and tremors. Volcanic activity and earthquakes can happen near Mt Elgon on the Kenya-Uganda border. Get to know earthquake safety measures for each place you stay.

themes.heading: Tsunamis
themes.text: Tsunamis can occur in Kenya's coastal areas. Move to high ground right away if local or regional authorities tell you to, or if you: feel a strong earthquake that makes it hard to stand up feel a weak, rolling earthquake that lasts a minute or more see a sudden rise or fall in sea level hear loud and unusual noises from the sea Don't wait for official warnings. Once on high ground, monitor local media.

themes.heading: Travel Insurance
themes.text: Get comprehensive travel insurance before you leave and closely read the Product Disclosure Statement (PDS) for what is and isn’t covered. It must cover all medical costs overseas, including evacuation. The Australian Government will not pay for these costs. If you can’t afford travel insurance, you shouldn’t travel. This is true for everyone, even if you are healthy and fit. If you don’t have insurance, you might have to pay thousands of dollars up front for medical care. Before you travel, check your policy, make sure: you know what activities and medical care your insurance covers, including evacuations your insurance covers you for your whole trip, including any stopovers you know any exclusions – for example, you might need to pay extra for activities like motorcycling, snow sports, scuba diving or bungee jumping. More information: Travel insurance

themes.heading: Physical and mental health
themes.text: Travelling overseas can be stressful and uncertain. You may experience unfamiliar environments, travel disruptions, changes in normal routines and limited access to health care. Not taking your prescribed medications is a common cause of mental ill-health for Australians overseas. If you are concerned about your welfare, or someone else's, contact your nearest Australian embassy, high commission, or consulate. You can also call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on +61 2 6261 3305. Mental health resources: Information and advice on mental illness (HealthDirect). Living with mental illness (BeyondBlue) Medicare Mental Health (the Department of Health and Aged Care) More information: General health advice Mental health support overseas

themes.heading: Ebola
themes.text: The World Health Organisation (WHO) declared an outbreak of Ebola disease in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) a public health emergency and also reported cases in Uganda. The Bundibugyo strain of Ebola has no approved vaccines or specific antiviral treatments. Ebola can be transmitted to humans through physical contact with an infectious person, infected animals or contaminated materials. Ebola is often fatal with a mortality rate of around 50 per cent. Symptoms of Ebola can include: high fever muscle pain and weakness headache sore throat vomiting and diarrhoea internal and external bleeding Seek medical advice immediately if you experience these symptoms. Maintain strict hygiene standards and avoid physical contact with anyone displaying symptoms. Health screening on arrival and entry restrictions for travellers who have visited countries affected by Ebola may be imposed with limited notice and can change quickly, including for transit passengers. Check the latest requirements with your airline or travel provider before departure. Further information and updates on Ebola can also be found on the World Health Organization (WHO) website.

themes.heading: Mpox
themes.text: There is currently an outbreak of Mpox in parts of Africa, including in Kenya. Mpox can be transmitted to humans through physical contact with an infectious person, infected animals or contaminated materials. Seek professional medical advice on Mpox vaccines. Maintain strict hygiene standards. Avoid physical contact with anyone who has Mpox. More information: Mpox

themes.heading: HIV/AIDS
themes.text: HIV/AIDS infection rates are high. Take precautions if taking part in activities that put you at risk of infection.

themes.heading: Insect-borne diseases
themes.text: Yellow fever is widespread in Kenya. Yellow fever is a potentially fatal virus spread by mosquitos. It's prevented by vaccination. Get vaccinated before you travel and take your yellow fever vaccination certificate with you to Kenya. Malaria is widespread except in Nairobi and at altitudes above 2500m. Other insect-borne diseases occur, including: dengue virus infection chikungunya lymphatic filariasis leishmaniasis Rift Valley fever African sleeping sickness To protect yourself from disease: make sure your accommodation is insect-proof use insect repellent wear long, loose, light-coloured clothing consider taking medication to prevent malaria Visit a doctor if you develop either a fever, muscle pain, a rash or a bad headache.

themes.heading: Other health risks
themes.text: Waterborne, foodborne and other infectious diseases are common. These include: hepatitis meningococcal disease measles cholera tuberculosis Serious outbreaks occur from time to time. To protect yourself from illness: make sure you get vaccinated against yellow fever, diphtheria and other illnesses before you travel drink boiled water or bottled water with sealed lids avoid ice cubes avoid raw and undercooked food, such as salads don't swim in fresh water Get medical advice if you have a fever or diarrhoea. Ask your doctor about health risks and vaccines before you go.

themes.heading: Medical care
themes.text: Medical facility standards vary. Medical facilities are adequate in urban areas but may be extremely limited in other places. Before receiving treatment, public and private facilities need: an up-front payment, or a payment guarantee, or medical insurance confirmation If you become seriously ill or injured in a remote area, you'll need to be evacuated to a major city. Medical evacuation can be very expensive. There's a decompression chamber at the Kenyan Naval Base in Mombasa.

themes.heading: Drugs
themes.text: Penalties for drug offences can be severe and include long jail terms. More information: Carrying or using drugs

themes.heading: LGBTQIA+ travellers
themes.text: Kenyan law prohibits same-sex relationships. Those convicted can face lengthy prison sentences. Avoid public displays of affection. More information: Advice for LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Local customs
themes.text: Kenya has conservative dress and behaviour standards, especially in coastal and rural areas. You should dress modestly; wear loose-fitting clothing that covers your shoulders, knees, midriff, chest and back. Take care not to offend. The Islamic holy month of Ramadan is observed in Kenya. Respect religious and cultural customs and laws at this time. Muslims don't eat, drink or smoke between sunrise and sunset during Ramadan. If you're not fasting, avoid these activities around people who are. Seek local advice to avoid offence. Public displays of affection can lead to harassment, particularly for same-sex couples. More information: Going overseas for major events

themes.heading: Visas and border measures
themes.text: Each country or territory decides its own entry and exit rules, including health border screening measures. To find out what you need to enter a destination, ask their embassy, consulate or immigration office.

themes.heading: Visas
themes.text: To enter Kenya you'll need to apply online and pay for an Electronic Travel Authorisation (ETA) prior to arrival. Check the ETA website for information on categories of persons exempted from applying for the ETA. For Kenyan visa and permit information, visit the ETA Kenya website. Entry and exit requirements can change at short notice. Contact the Kenyan High Commission in Australia for details about ETAs, currency, customs and quarantine rules.

themes.heading: Yellow fever vaccination
themes.text: Yellow Fever is widespread. If you're coming from a yellow fever-endemic country, you'll need a yellow fever vaccination certificate to enter Kenya, or transit through Kenya for 12 hours or longer. Some airlines may want to see the certificate when you leave. Find out about returning to Australia after exposure to yellow fever. More information: Yellow fever

themes.heading: Lost or stolen passport
themes.text: Your passport is valuable and can be used by others to commit crimes. Don’t let anyone trick you into giving it to them. Always keep it in a safe place. If your passport is lost or stolen, tell the Australian Government: In Australia: contact the Australian Passport Information Service. Overseas: contact the nearest Australian embassy or consulate.

themes.heading: Passport with 'X' gender identifier
themes.text: Australian passports comply with international standards for sex and gender. If your passport has an 'X' in the sex field, we can’t guarantee your entry or transit to other countries. Ask the embassy or consulate of your destinations if they’ll accept your passport with an 'X' gender marker. Check all transit destinations too. More information: LGBTQIA+ travellers

themes.heading: Money
themes.text: The local currency is the Kenyan Shilling (KES). More information: Kenya Shilling banknotes (Central Bank) Declare all amounts over US$10,000 when you arrive and depart. This covers all forms of currency, not only cash. Large banks and foreign exchange bureaus continue to accept traveller's cheques, but they can be difficult to cash and attract high fees. Hotels don't usually accept them. ATMs in Nairobi and major towns accept international credit cards. Take care when paying with credit cards or using ATMs. Card skimming incidents are increasing. Only use ATMs at large shopping centres or in banks. Check the machine for unusual parts before you use it. Always keep your card in sight during transactions. Ask your bank if your cards will work in Kenya. M-PESA is a common form of electronic funds transfer accepted across Kenya, including at national parks. National parks do not accept cash and generally accept credit cards, but at times, due to technical difficulties, only payment via M-PESA is accepted.

themes.heading: Driving permit
themes.text: To drive in Kenya, you'll need both: a valid Australian driver's licence (digital driver's licences may not be accepted) an International Driving Permit (IDP) Get your IDP before leaving Australia.

themes.heading: Road travel
themes.text: Kenya records thousands of road fatalities each year. You're more likely to die in a motor vehicle accident in Kenya than in Australia. Hazards include: poor road conditions unsafe and poorly maintained vehicles not enough street lighting Before you drive: get to know local traffic laws and practices check local information on road conditions, including security risks and road closures avoid travel at night on major highways in and out of Nairobi and on rural roads

themes.heading: Motorcycles
themes.text: Motorcycle accidents are common. Check your travel insurance policy covers you when using a motorbike or similar vehicle. Always wear a helmet. More information: Driving or riding

themes.heading: Taxis
themes.text: Only use established and licensed taxis and limousine services. You can arrange these through your hotel. Only use taxis from official taxi stands or via callout. When travelling at night to and from Nairobi's airports (JKIA and Wilson), always use reputable tour group or taxi companies. App-based transport services are available in Nairobi, Mombasa and other major cities. Only use reputable rideshare services booked through official apps.

themes.heading: Public transport
themes.text: Public transport options such as buses and minivans ('matatus') are dangerous. Australian officials in Kenya don't use public buses and minivans or motorcycle taxis ('boda boda') due to safety concerns. Bus terminals and other transport hubs have been targeted in terrorist and criminal attacks. There are risks of further attacks. Theft is common on many train routes. Passengers' belongings have been taken from their compartments. Watch your belongings at all times.

themes.heading: Air travel
themes.text: DFAT doesn’t give advice about how safe different airlines or flight paths are. Safety risks and weather events may cause flight delays and cancellations. Contact your travel agent or airline to confirm your plans. Airlines and airports can restrict what you can take in your luggage. Regulations may be different to those in Australia. Check with your airline before you travel. Strikes at Jomo Kenyatta International Airport in Nairobi can occur with little notice. Check airline websites for any delays or check with your travel provider. Get to the airport early to ensure you have enough time to clear security and immigration. Check Kenya's air safety profile with the Aviation Safety Network. More information: Air travel

themes.heading: Consular contacts
themes.text: The Australian Government aims to provide high quality consular assistance. There are limits to what we can do. Read the Consular Services Charter to understand how we can assist you overseas. For consular assistance, contact the Australian High Commission in Nairobi.

themes.heading: 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre
themes.text: In a consular emergency, if you can't contact an embassy, call the 24-hour Consular Emergency Centre on: +61 2 6261 3305 from overseas 1300 555 135 in Australia

themes.text:

[케냐] 케냐 정부 운영 '재난안전 모바일앱' 정보 2026-07-03

themes.heading: 대사관 연락처
themes.text: ㅇ 주소 : 1st, 2 nd Fl. Misha Tower, Westlands Road, Nairobi, Kenya ㅇ 대표번호(근무시간 중) : (254) 20-361-5000 ㅇ 긴급연락처(사건사고 등 긴급상황 발생 시, 24시간) : (254) 708-984-891 ㅇ 이메일 : emb-ke@mofa.go.kr

themes.heading: 주재국 신고
themes.text: ㅇ 범죄, 화재 또는 긴급 구조요청은 999입니다. 휴대폰을 사용할 경우에는 020-999, 로밍폰을 사용할 경우에는 +254-20-999로 전화하시면 됩니다.

themes.heading: 관광통역서비스
themes.text: ㅇ 여행객들을 위한 별도의 관광 및 통역서비스는 없고, 한인여행사 등에 의뢰하면 한국인 가이드(전문 가이드는 아니고, 장기간 케냐에 거주한 교민)를 구할 수 있습니다.

themes.heading: 의료기관 연락처
themes.text: ㅇ 나이로비의 경우 동부아프리카 지역 내에서는 비교적 일정수준의 시설을 갖춘 병원(Nairobi hospital, Aga Khan 등)이 있으며, 나이로비 외 지역은 소규모 병원이 지역별로 있으나 외국인이 사용하기 불편하고 응급처치 정도만 되는 수준 ㅇ 여행객 또는 현지 거주 외국인이 치료를 받고자 할 경우 많은 비용이 소요되며, 특히 입원할 경우 엄청난 보증금(deposit)을 요구하는 경향이 있습니다. 퇴원시 보증금의 대부분이 치료비로 청구(청구서 내역을 세밀히 확인해야 함)되고, 보증금을 반환 받기도 매우 어려우므로 입원시 보증금에 대하여 적절하게 협상을 하는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 주요 병원 연락처 Nairobi hospital - (020) 2845000 Aga Khan - (020) 3662000, Kenyatta Hospital - (020) 2726300 ㅇ 나이로비외 지역에서 사고 발생시에는 응급처치만 하고, 차량이 없는 경우 나이로비 지역으로 환자를 운송하는 앰블런스를 요청하는 것이 필요 - ST. Johns Ambulance : (020) 334 02 62/74/83 - AMREF Flying Doctor Service : (020) 6992299 / 3315455 / 6002492 - Intensive Care Air Ambulance : (020) 600-4945 - AAR Emergency Ambuance : (020) 2717374

themes.heading: 기본정보 (basisinformatie)
themes.text: ㅇ 수 도 : 나이로비 ㅇ 면 적 : 57만㎢ (남한의 약6배) ㅇ 민족구성 : Kikuyu(23%), Luhya(14%), Luo(13%), Kalenjin(11%), Kamba(10%), Kisii(8%), Meru(8%) 등 약 44개 부족 ㅇ 종 교 : 기독교(80%, 카톨릭포함), 이슬람(10%), 기타(10%) ㅇ 언 어 : 스와힐리어(통용어), 영어(공용어) ㅇ 통화단위 : Kenya Shilling(Ksh) (1USD = 약 128Ksh)

themes.heading: 치안환경
themes.text: ㅇ 케냐의 사법 및 치안시스템은 대한민국과 달라 일단 사건이 발생하면 피해회복 또는 범인검거가 어렵기 때문에 무엇보다 예방이 중요합니다. ㅇ 또한 케냐 법집행기관 특히, 경찰은 부정부패 등으로 인해 신고를 해도 수사에 미온적이거나 수사를 조건으로 착수비 등 대가를 요구하는 경우가 다반사임을 유념하셔야 합니다. ㅇ 케냐 경찰은 오히려 검문검색을 빙자하여 이유없이 외국인을 체포하고 석방조건으로 수백달러의 금품을 갈취하는 사례도 빈발하므로 잘 모르는 지역을 이동할 때에는 지인 또는 현지인을 대동하여야 하며 여행객의 경우 야간 도보 이동을 절대 삼가야 합니다.

themes.heading: 사건ㆍ사고의 유형
themes.text: ㅇ 케냐는 소말리아, 우간다, 탄자니아, 수단과 인접하고 국경경비가 취약하여 총기유입 및 마약류 유입이 많고 경기침체로 인한 강도, 절도, 납치 등 강력사건이 빈발하고 있어 심지어 수도 나이로비(Nairobi)는 나이로버리(Nairobbery)라는 별칭이 있을 정도이기 때문에 여행객은 각별히 유의해야 합니다. ㅇ 과거에는 강도들이 현금, 휴대폰, 귀중품들을 강탈해 가는 것으로 그쳤으나 이를 거부하는 경우 피해자를 살해하는 경우도 있습니다. 따라서, 강도를 당한 경우에는 강도들을 쳐다보거나 절대 머뭇거리지 않아야 하며 강도들의 요구에 순순히 응하여야 합니다. ㅇ 절도와 관련하여 차량을 운전하시거나 주차하실 때에는 창문을 열지 않아야 차량 내 절도를 예방할 수 있으며 길거리를 이동할 때에는 오토바이 날치기를 유의해야 합니다. 또한 도보 이동시에는 휴대전화를 하는 경우 이를 날치기 하는 경우도 있기 때문에 도보 이동시 휴대전화를 하는 것을 삼가야 하며 호텔 내 커피숍에서도 반드시 핸드백은 등 뒤에 두거나 시야 앞에 두어야 하며 화장실을 이용할 때에도 이를 휴대하여야 합니다. ㅇ 케냐에서는 콩고인, 탄자니아인 등이 연루되어 금을 수출한다는 명목으로 대금만을 편취하는 사기가 발생하고 있습니다. 이들은 모든 통관서류를 위조하며 물품이 선적되었으나 사법당국의 제지 또는 유엔의 제지 등으로 인해 운송되지 못하였다는 등 터무니 없는 변명을 사기의 수법으로 사용하기 때문에 섣불리 물품대금을 먼저 송금하는 일이 결코 없도록 하여야 합니다. (많은 피해자들이 실제 금샘플에 대한 순도측정에서 진품임을 확인한 후에는 의심을 풀게 되는 경향이 있는데 이곳 사기범들은 심지어 경찰, 세관과도 공모하여 서류를 위조하는 경우가 있음을 명심하셔야 합니다.) ※ 범죄가 발생하면 피해 회복이 거의 불가능하므로 사전에 충분한 예방조치를 취하는 것이 최선임을 명심하시기 바랍니다. [테러 관련 동향] ㅇ 2019.1.15.(화) 15:30경 나이로비 소재 호텔에서 테러 발생, 테러단체 알샤밥의 소행으로 20여명이 사망하였습니다.(한국인은 없음) ㅇ 2015. 4. 2 새벽 5:30경 소말리아 국경으로부터 150km 떨어져 있는 가리사 대학에서 소말리아 테러단체 알사밥에 의한 테러로 인해 케냐인 148명이 사망하는 테러가 발생하였습니다. ㅇ 2013. 9.21 나이로비의 웨스트게이트 쇼핑몰에서 소말리아의 무장단체 알샤바브에 의해 테러가 발생하였으며 우리 국민 1명을 포함 총 71명이 사망하는 사건이 발생하였습니다. ㅇ 2011년 10월 케냐 정부가 소말리아에 군대를 파견한 이후 알사밥은 지속적으로 케냐를 상대로 테러를 자행해 오고 있으며 2014년 12월 이후부터 가리사 사태까지 테러 공격 횟수가 증가하고 있는 추세이므로 소말리아 국경 및 해안으로의 이동을 자제하여야 하며 수도인 나이로비에서도 다중밀집 시설에의 장기 체류는 항상 유의하여야 합니다.

themes.heading: 자연재해
themes.text: ㅇ 케냐는 기후가 매우 쾌적한 나라이며 지진이나 태풍 등 자연재해는 없으나 건기에는 일부지역에 가뭄으로 인한 물부족 현상이 있기도 합니다. 다만 최근 기후변화의 영향으로 열대성 폭우가 발생하여 홍수에 의한 피해도 생기는 만큼 주의가 요망됩니다.

themes.heading: 유의해야할 지역
themes.text: ㅇ 나이로비 내 키베라 등 슬럼지역은 아주 위험하므로 이곳에 대한 출입은 자제해야 합니다. 업무, 봉사활동 등으로 부득이 방문하는 경우에도 불필요한 사진 촬영 등으로 시비에 휘말리지 않도록 주의하여야 합니다.

themes.heading: 출입국 (in- en uitreis)
themes.text: 무비자 입국 가능 여부(1회 입국시 최대 연속 체류 가능 일자) ㅇ 외교관 여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 관용여권 소지자 : X ㅇ 일반여권 소지자(복수와 단수를 구분할 경우 별도 표기 필요) : X ㅇ 도착사증 발급 여부 : 도착사증 미발급 - 입국전 Electornic Travel Authorization(eTA) 싸이트를 통해서 사전 승인을 받아야함 (www.etakenya.go.ke/en) * 여행 Visa라는 개념이 없어졌으며, 상기 eTA로 대체 입국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 공항, 관공서내 혹은 관공서 밀집 도심에서 사진촬영불가 ㅇ 공항내에 지정된 장소에서만 흡연 ㅇ 비닐봉지 사용금지 (케냐 전지역) 출국시 유의사항 등 ㅇ 미화 $10,000 이상의 외국환 반출시 사전 시고 필요 참고자료 ㅇ 해당 국가의 주한공관홈페이지 ㅇ 해당국가의 한국 대사관 홈페이지 : www.overseas.mofa.go.kr/ke-ko/index.do ㅇ 기타 참고자료 : - ETA 신청싸이트 : www.etakenya.go.ke/en

themes.heading: 일반문화
themes.text: ㅇ 케냐인들은 큰소리로 화내는 것을 매우 싫어하며 인격 모독으로 받아들이는 경우가 많아 특히 주의해야 합니다. 케냐인들의 일처리나 태도가 매우 느려 이에 대한 불만으로 큰 목소리로 항의하거나 화를 낼 경우 오히려 케냐인들을 분노하게 하여 상황을 더 어렵게 하는 경우가 종종 있습니다. ㅇ 케냐인들은 쉽게 ‘Hello, my friend’ 하고 친근감을 표시하며 자신의 요구사항을 말하므로 이 말에 현혹되지 않도록 주의해야 하며, 단순한 인사 정도로 받아들이는 것이 좋습니다. ㅇ 어려운 경제상황 및 생활수준으로 인하여 처음 보는 사람한테도 자신의 어려운 처지를 설명하며 금전적인 도움을 요청하는 경우가 많으나 적절하게 거절하는 것이 다른 한국인들에 대한 인식을 위해서도 바람직 합니다.

themes.heading: 종교관련
themes.text: ㅇ 케냐는 개인의 종교의 자유가 보장되고, 외국인들의 종교 활동에 특별한 제한은 없습니다. 케냐인들은 개신교 45%, 카톨릭 33%, 무슬림 10%, 전통종교 10%, 기타 2% 등으로 구성되어 있으며, 현지 교민들은 개인별 종교에 따라 한인교회 또는 한인성당에 다니고 있으며, 위 교민들 중 절반 정도는 선교사로 활동하고 있습니다.

themes.heading: 팁 문화
themes.text: ㅇ 요금에 보통 팁이 포함되어 있지 않으므로 종업원 또는 도우미 1인당 50-100케냐실링 정도(또는 1 USD)의 팁을 주는 것이 보편화되어 있습니다.

themes.heading: 주요 교통 법규 및 문화
themes.text: [안전벨트] ㅇ 자동차를 운전할 시는 반드시 안전벨트를 착용해야 한다. ㅇ 운전자와 승객은 모두 안전벨트를 착용하는 것이 합법적 요구사항이다. [갖추어야 할 서류] ㅇ 반드시 각 차종에 맞는 합법적인 운전면허증을 소지해야 한다. ㅇ 반드시 차량보험에 가입하여 유효한 차량보험증 또는 최소한 제 3자를 위해 든 보험증을 가지고 있어야 한다. ㅇ 자동차 등록서류를 가지고 있어야 한다. ㅇ 여권사본과 ID card를 항상 소지하여 신분 확인시 보여 주어야 한다. [속도 제한] ㅇ 나이로비 시내 내에서 속도 제한은 50 km/h 이며, 나이로비 외곽 시내 및 도시 속도제한은 100 km/h 임 ㅇ 케냐에서는 차량 추월시 외에는 반드시 왼쪽 운전임 ㅇ 도로 상황과 날씨(특히 폭우와 안개) 및 자신이 운전하는 차종을 감안하여 속도를 조절하여야 한다. ㅇ 항상 교통의 상황의 흐름에 따라 속도를 조절해야 한다. ㅇ 모든 견인하는 차량의 경우에는 반드시 차량 앞에 잘 보이도 크게 “T” 싸인을 붙여야 한다. [교통 신호등] ㅇ 항상 교통 신호등을 지켜 안전을 지킨다. ㅇ 교통신호등은 주로 영국 식민지 시절에 영국 시스템으로 설치되어 있으며, 일반적으로 시내 및 주요도로에만 설치되어 있다. ㅇ 운전자와 보행자는 반드시 교통 신호등을 잘 보고 교통 신호등 법규를 잘 지켜야 하며, 법규를 어겨 도로를 건너거나 운전하면 안된다. ㅇ 녹색 : 보행자가 도로를 걸어간다. 운전자는 정지한다. ㅇ 빨강색 : 운전자는 운전하여 지나가며, 보행자는 정지한다. ㅇ 노랑색 : 보행자 또는 운전자가 건널 준비를 한다. [운전 허용 나이] ㅇ 케냐에서 운전 허용 최소한의 나이는 오토바이 또는 자동차의 경우 18세, 밴 또는 트력의 경우는 24세이다. ㅇ 자동차를 임대할 경우에는 2년이상 자동차 운전면허증 소지자로서 23세이상이어야 한다. [안전 카메라 장치] ㅇ 차량 속도는 도로마다 설치된 안전 카메라 장치에 의해 체크되며, 케냐 교통안전청에 제시된 법규에 따라 도로에 표시된 속도제한 표지판 속도에 따른다. [도로 현장에서 벌금을 고지받은 경우] ㅇ 도로에서 교통법규 위반으로 벌금을 고지받은 경우에는 경찰관이 고지한 “임시 법정”에 가서 알려준 벌금을 내야 한다. [운전시 핸드폰을 사용할 경우] ㅇ 운전자는 운전중에 핸드폰을 사용할 수 없다. 단, Hand free kit를 가지고 사용할 경우는 무방하다. ㅇ 차량 안의 승객은 운전자를 방해하는 장치들(devices)을 사용해서는 안된다. [어린이 안전 규칙] ㅇ 12세 이하 어린이는 반드시 자동차 앞좌석에 앉히면 안된다. 또한 가능한 나이어린 어린이들은 뒷좌석에 안전하게 잘 앉히도록 한다. [음주운전 금지] ㅇ 음주운전은 금물이다. ㅇ 졸음 운전을 금한다. – 졸음 운전시 사고 확률이 높고 음주 운전과 같이 굉장히 위험하다. ㅇ 알코올 혈중 농도는 80 mg per 100ml 초과시 경찰관에 의해 연행되거나 법정에서 거액의 벌금을 물게 되며, 차량은 압수된다.

themes.heading: 관련 사건사고 발생시 대처방법
themes.text: [차량 사고] ㅇ 모든 차량사고는 경찰에게 신고하며, 신고 번호는 999이다. ㅇ 경미한 차량 사고인 경우는 사고 상대방과 협의하여 해결할 수 있다. [주차 법규] ㅇ 아주 작은 마을의 경우 주차비의 지불없이 주차할 수 있는 곳에 주차하면 된다. ㅇ 나이로비 시내 등 도시에서는 반드시 주차할 수 있는 곳에 주차하고 주차비를 지불해야 한다. 그렇지 않을 경우에는 주차 단속반에 의해 차량을 사용하지 못하도록 차에 족쇠를 채운다. [주차료] ㅇ 일반적으로 나이로비 시내 및 도시에서는 차를 주차할 수 있는 곳은 주차 공간이 정해져 있으며 주차비의 경우는 주차 장소에 따라 200 케냐실링에서 1,000 케냐실링 임 ㅇ 나이로비 시내 및 주요 도시에서는 하루동안 공용주차장에서 주차하는 경우 일반적으로 300케냐실링을 지불하며 지역에 따라 차이가 있다. [KAPS A-CARD] ㅇ 오토바이 운전자의 경우는 KAPS A-CARD를 발급받아 오토바이 주차비를 시간과 장소에 따라 이에 합당한 주차비를 지불한다. [주차관련 법률 시행] ㅇ 주차에 관한 법규는 경찰이나 사설 주차 공간의 소유주에 의해 시행된다. ㅇ 나이로비내의 주차비 관련사항은 주정부(County government)가 담당하고 있다. [주차] ㅇ 장애인을 위한 주차 공간은 주차 공간 안에 반드시 특정한 장소에 마련되어 있으며, 주차 공간에 장애인 주차공간임을 알리는 그림이 그려져 있다.

themes.heading: 기타 유의사항
themes.text: ㅇ 교통위반에 대한 케냐교통안전청(NTSA)의 벌금 내역 https://informationcradle.com/kenya/wp-content/uploads/2013/08/FIRST-SCHEDULE.pdf (Traffic Amendment Act 2014)

themes.heading: 교통정보
themes.text: ㅇ 우버(UBER) 및 택시파이(Taxify) 나이로비 및 주요도시에서 영업을 시작하여, 일반 택시에 비해 편리하고 저렴하게 이용할 수 있음.(다만, 일몰 이후 사용 자제 등 이용 시 항상 개별 안전 주의 요망) ㅇ 시내 방문시 외국인 여행객은 주로 택시를 이용할 수밖에 없는데, 상가나 관공서 주변에 대기 중인 택시를 이용할 수도 있으나 호텔 프런트에 요청해서 택시를 이용하는 것이 안전하며, 가정에서는 콜택시를 이용할 것을 권장합니다. - 콜택시는 JIM CAB, JATCO가 가장 안전한 것으로 알려져 있습니다. ※ JIM CAB : 712-2565, 0733-735499 ※ JATCO : 0733-701494 ※ WONDERVIEW : 0733-678953 - 요금은 미터기를 사용하지 않으므로 보통 흥정하여 결정하나, 도심지 내 주행의 경우 통상적으로 500～1,000 실링 정도, 공항에서 나이로비 도심지까지(약20km, 교통체증 없을 시 30분소요) 1,500～2,000 실링정도 소요됩니다. ㅇ "마타투"라고 하는 버스는 현지인들의 중요한 교통수단이지만 난폭 운전 등으로 교통사고의 위험이 많고, 버스 내에서 소매치기 등이 자주 발생하므로 가급적 이용하지 않는 것이 바람직합니다. 일반적인 경미한 교통법규 위반에 대한 벌금 내역: 1. 자동차 번호판을 부착하지 않았을 시 – Sh 10,000 2. 운전시 교통 신호를 보내 확인하지 않았을 경우 - Sh 3,000 3. 경찰 정장을 입은 경찰관이 정지하도록 명령하였으나 이를 따르지 않았을 경우 – Sh 5,000 4. 경찰 정장을 입은 경찰관이 구두 또는 신호로 임무 수행을 위해 지시한 방향으로 가라고 했는데 이를 어겼을 경우 가지 않았을 경우 – Sh 3,000 5. 차종에 맞는 유효한 운전면허증을 소지하지 않고 운전하는 경우 – Sh 7,000 6. 운전면허증이 유효하지 않거나 기간이 만료된 경우 – Sh 1,000 7. 운전 차량에 경고 표지판 등을 소지하지 않은 경우 – Sh 2,000 8. 차량 운전시 안전벨트를 하지 않은 경우 – Sh 500 9. 공용서비스차량(PSV) 운전시 운전면허증이나 차량등록증이 적법하지 않을 경우 – Sh 7,000 10. 승합 버스나 마타투(케냐 공용 밴) 운전시 합법적으로 정해진 버스 정류장이나 터미날에서 승객을 태워야 하는데 그렇지 않고 비합법적인 장소에서 승객을 태웠을 경우 – Sh 3,000 11. 승객을 강제로 태우고자 하는 경우 – Sh 3,000 12. 차량에 탑승시 신체 일부를 차량 밖으로 내 놓는 경우 – Sh 1,000 13. 승객을 합법적으로 정해진 버스 정류장이나 터미날에 내리지 않고 비합법적인 장소에서 승객을 내리게 하는 경우 – Sh 1,000 14. 정해진 속도를 유지하지 않고 위반하는 경우 – Sh 10,000 15. 차량 소유자가 안전벨트를 잘 사용할 수 있도록 깨끗하고 안전하게 유지하지 않았을 경우 – Sh 2,000 16. 시내 중심지에서 속도제한 50KPH를 초과하는 경우 – sh.25,000 차량 속도는 도로마다 설치된 안전 카메라 장치에 의해 체크되며, 표시된 속도제한 표지판 속도에 따른다. ㅇ It will start at City Cabanas on Mombasa Road, the KWS gate on Lang’ata Road, Nakumatt Junction ong Ngong’ Road and from Nakumatt Junction to the Uthiru turn-off on Naivasha Road. ㅇ The limit begins at the junction of James Gichuru Road along Waiyaki Way, at Mobil Petrol Station on Peponi Road and the Muthaiga Mini Market on Limuru Road. ㅇ On the Northern by-pass, the application of the rule starts at the Nyayo Barracks roundabout, and from Ridgeways on Kimabu Road. 케냐 도로에서 어떻게 운전할 것인지에 대한 일반적인 조언 I. 상기에서 언급한 모든 도로 교통 법규를 지킨다. II. 운전시에는 반드시 모든 문을 잠근다. 단, 신선한 공기를 위해서는 조금만 창문을 열어서 도둑이 귀중품을 소매치기하지 못하도록 한다. III. 고속도로(highway)에서 승객을 태우지 않는다. 승객을 태울 경우 거지, 노인, 환자, 장애인, 임산부 등을 가장하여 차를 강탈(carjackers) 하거나 납치범(hijackers)일 가능성이 많다. IV. 운전시, 주위에서 타이어가 펑크가 났다고 말할때 그 자리에서 바로 차를 정지하지 말고 가까운 주유소 등에 가서 체크후 도움을 받는다. V. 운전시 고속도로(highway) 또는 밤에 뒷 차량이 차를 받았을 때 차 에서 밖으로 나오지 않고 곧장 가까운 경찰서에 가서 리포트한다. 이런 대부분의 경우 납치범(hijackers)일 가능성이 많다. VI. 낯선 사람에게서 물건을 사거나 명함을 받는 것을 회피해야 한다. 그렇지 않으면 산 물건이나 받은 명함에 약품이 묻어 있어 기절하게 하여 가지고 있는 물건등을 훔쳐가기 때문이다. VII. Heavy traffic is experienced between 7:00 am – 9:00 am, 12:00pm -1:30pm and 4:00 – 7:00pm

themes.heading: 날씨
themes.text: ㅇ 적도를 통과하고 있으나, 동부해안 지방과 북부 사막지대를 제외한 대부분이 고원 평원지대로 한국의 초가을 날씨처럼 낮에만 덥고 아침과 저녁에는 선선한 날씨입니다. ㅇ 연평균 기온 - 해안(20.5-32도) - 내륙(9.5-23.6도) - 고원지방 및 서부는 강우 풍부, 북부는 건조 - 2~3월중 기온최고, 7-8월중 기온최저

themes.heading: 기타
themes.text: ㅇ 시차 : 한국보다 6시간 늦습니다. ㅇ 2021.1.1.부터 케냐 입국 비자는 100% e-visa 발급만을 통해서 비자 취득이 가능(비지니스 목적으로 방문할 경우 영문 출장 명령서를 첨부하여 상용비자를 받아야 함) ※ 사전비자취득 정보 : http://ecitizen.go.ke 1) 위 사이트에 접속하여 우선 회원 등록을 해야 합니다. 2) 'Visitor' 자격으로 회원등록을 합니다. 3) 로그인 후에 'Immigration' 서비스를 선택합니다. 4) 'Submit Application' 항목을 선택합니다. 5) 'Kenya Visa' 항목을 선택합니다. 이때 반드시 방문목적에 맞는 비자 형식을 클릭하여 신청하셔야 합니다. 6) 신청서식을 작성해 넣습니다. 7) 신용카드로 수수료를 결재합니다(수수료는 환불되지 않습니다). 8) 승인을 기다립니다(7일 정도 소요되며 이메일을 통해 승인 메일을 받게 됩니다). 9) 승인 후에 'eVisa'를 출력하고 공항 입국 시 출력물을 이민국 직원에게 제출합니다. (출력물은 아직 정식비자가 아니며, 입국 시 반드시 이민국 직원에게 보여 주어야 합니다) - 체류 중 비자 기간이 넘지 않도록 주의 필요. 비자기간 경과 후 체류 시 이민국에 체포되어 재판을 받게 됨 (관광비자를 가지고 기타 사업 목적으로 여행할 경우 단속의 대상이 되므로 주의를 요함) ㅇ 국적과 관계없이 황열병 우려 지역으로부터 입국하는 사람에게 황열병 예방접종확인서(Yellow card)를 요구하며, 예방접종이 되지 않았을 경우 입국 거부될 수 있습니다. - 아프리카 여러 나라를 방문하는 경우 국내에서 황열병 예방접종을 하고 출국하는 것이 바람직합니다.
